props_visible: Ornate mabkhara (incense burner) — hand-crafted, studded with golden beads/dots in intricate patterns, hourglass form
Traditional dallah rack (wooden tripod with tassels in red, yellow, blue)
Aseeb staff uniform (white vest/blazer over black thobe, red-white ghutrah)

characters_visible.wardrobe_notes: White fitted waistcoat/blazer over dark thobe, red-and-white ghutrah — branded staff uniform
characters_visible.gesture_notes: Right hand holding mabkhara handle — object presented forward toward viewer

notable_visual_elements: Mabkhara is a masterpiece of traditional Saudi craft — the studded bead work is elaborate and expensive
Presenting the mabkhara toward camera = 'this is offered to you' hospitality gesture
Incense (bukhoor/oud) service is a traditional Saudi welcome ritual before or after meals
Uniform shows brand has standardized staff presentation at professional level
Name badge visible — staff are named, known individuals, not anonymous workers

hospitality_cues: Mabkhara (incense burner) with bukhoor/oud = quintessential Saudi welcome and farewell ritual
Offering incense to guests is one of the most culturally specific Saudi hospitality acts — not shared with other global restaurant cultures
cultural_notes.free_notes: The inclusion of incense service (mabkhara) in the restaurant experience is a uniquely Saudi hospitality differentiator. This act — wafting oud/bukhoor over guests — is performed in Saudi homes at the end of gathering as a signal of the host's generosity and the guest's honored status. @aseeb.najd including this in their content is communicating: we offer the full Najdi hospitality protocol, not just food.

pattern_matches.notes: Ornate mabkhara as hero — presented to camera as an offering, representing incense hospitality ritual
Incense service is a distinctly Saudi hospitality signal; showing it claims cultural authenticity
